title: Necessary and sufficient graphical conditions for optimal adjustment sets in causal graphical models with hidden variables [PDF]

paragraphs.text: ## **Necessary and sufficient graphical conditions for optimal adjustment sets in causal graphical models with hidden variables**

## **Jakob Runge**

German Aerospace Center Institute of Data Science 07745 Jena, Germany and Technische Universität Berlin 10623 Berlin, Germany `jakob.runge@dlr.de`

## **Abstract**

The problem of selecting optimal backdoor adjustment sets to estimate causal effects in graphical models with hidden and conditioned variables is addressed. Previous work has defined optimality as achieving the smallest asymptotic estimation variance and derived an optimal set for the case without hidden variables. For the case with hidden variables there can be settings where no optimal set exists and currently only a sufficient graphical optimality criterion of limited applicability has been derived. In the present work optimality is characterized as maximizing a certain adjustment information which allows to derive a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set and a definition and algorithm to construct it. Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has higher (or equal) adjustment information than the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [Journal of Machine Learning Research, 18: 1–62, 2018] for any graph. The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation. Numerical experiments indicate that the asymptotic results also hold for relatively small sample sizes and that the optimal adjustment set or minimized variants thereof often yield better variance also beyond that estimator class. Surprisingly, among the randomly created setups more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions indicating that also in many real-world scenarios graphical optimality may hold.

## **1 Introduction**

A standard problem setting in causal inference is to estimate the causal effect between two variables given a causal graphical model that specifies qualitative causal relations among observed variables [Pearl, 2009], including a possible presence of hidden confounding variables. The graphical model then allows to employ graphical criteria to identify valid adjustment sets, the most well-known being the _backdoor criterion_ [Pearl, 1993] and the _generalized adjustment criterion_ [Shpitser et al., 2010, Perkovi´c et al., 2015, 2018], providing a complete identification of all valid adjustment sets. Estimators of causal effects based on such a valid adjustment set as a covariate are then unbiased, but for different adjustment sets the estimation variance may strongly vary. An _optimal adjustment set_ may be characterized as one that has minimal asymptotic estimation variance. In **current work** , following Kuroki and Cai [2004] and Kuroki and Miyakawa [2003], Henckel et al. [2019] (abbreviated

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

HPM19 in the following) showed that graphical optimality always holds for linear models in the causally sufficient case where all relevant variables are observed. In Witte et al. [2020] an alternative characterization of the optimal adjustment set is discussed and the approach was integrated into the IDA algorithm [Maathuis et al., 2009, 2010] that does not require the causal graph to be known. Rotnitzky and Smucler [2019] extended the results in HPM19 to asymptotically linear non-parametric graphical models. HPM19’s optimal adjustment set holds for the causally sufficient case (no hidden variables) and the authors gave an example with hidden variables where optimality does not hold in general, i.e., the optimal adjustment set depends on the coefficients and noise terms (more generally, the distribution), rather than just the graph. Most recently, Smucler et al. [2021] (SSR20) partially extended these results to the non-parametric hidden variables case together with _dynamic treatment regimes_ , i.e., conditional causal effects. SSR20 provide a sufficient criterion for an optimal set to exist and a definition based on a certain undirected graph-construction using a result by van der Zander et al. [2019]. However, their sufficient criterion is very restrictive and a current major open problem is a _necessary_ and sufficient condition for an optimal adjustment set to exist in the hidden variable case and a corresponding definition of an optimal set.

My **main theoretical contribution** is a solution to this problem. Optimality for conditional causal effects in the hidden variables case is fully characterized by an information-theoretic approach involving a certain difference of conditional mutual informations among the observed variables termed the adjustment information. Maximizing the adjustment information formalizes the common intuition to choose adjustment sets that maximally constrain the effect variable and minimally constrain the cause variable. This allows to derive a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set. The derived optimal adjustment set also has the property of minimum cardinality, i.e., no node can be removed without sacrificing optimality. Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has higher (or equal) adjustment information than the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not. The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation that, at present, I could only verify theoretically for the linear case. As **practical contributions** the paper provides extensive numerical experiments that corroborate the theoretical results and show that the optimal adjustment set or minimized variants thereof often yield better variance also beyond the theoretically analyzed estimator class. Code is available in the python package `https://github.com/jakobrunge/tigramite` . More detailed preliminaries, proofs, algorithms, and further numerical experiments are given in the Supplementary Material.

## **1.1 Preliminaries and problem setting**

We consider causal effects in causal graphical models over a set of variables **V** with a joint distribution _P_ = _P_ ( **V** ) that is consistent with an acyclic directed mixed graph (ADMG) _G_ = ( **V** _, E_ ). Two nodes can have possibly more than one edge which can be _directed_ ( _←_ ) or _bi-directed_ ( _↔_ ). See Fig. 1A for an example. Kinships are defined as usual: parents _pa_ ( _X_ ) for “ _•→X_ ”, spouses _sp_ ( _X_ ) for “ _X↔•_ ”, children _ch_ ( _X_ ) for “ _X→•_ ”. These sets all exclude _X_ . Correspondingly descendants _des_ ( _X_ ) and ancestors _an_ ( _X_ ) are defined, which, on the other hand, both include _X_ . The mediator nodes on causal paths from _X_ to _Y_ are denoted **M** = **M** ( _X, Y_ ) and exclude _X_ and _Y_ . For detailed preliminaries, including the definition of open and blocked paths, see Supplementary Section A. In this work we only consider a univariate intervention variable _X_ and effect variable _Y_ . We simplify set notation and denote unions of variables as _{W } ∪_ **M** _∪_ **A** = _W_ **MA** .

A (possibly empty) set of adjustment variables **Z** for the total causal effect of _X_ on _Y_ in an ADMG is called _valid_ relative to ( _X, Y_ ) if the interventional distribution for setting _do_ ( _X_ = **x** ) [Pearl, 2009] factorizes as _p_ ( _Y |do_ ( _X_ = **x** )) = � _p_ ( _Y |_ **x** _,_ **z** ) _p_ ( **z** ) _d_ **z** for non-empty **Z** and as _p_ ( _Y |do_ ( _X_ = **x** )) = _p_ ( _Y |_ **x** ) for empty **Z** . Valid adjustment sets, the set of which is here denoted _Z_ , can be read off from a given causal graph using the generalized adjustment criterion [Perkovi´c et al., 2015, 2018] which generalizes Pearl’s back-door criterion [Pearl, 2009]. A set **Z** is valid if both of the following conditions hold: (i) **Z** _∩_ **forb** = _∅_ , and (ii) all non-causal paths from _X_ to _Y_ are blocked by **Z** . An adjustment set is called _minimal_ if no strict subset of **Z** is still valid. We assume a non-zero causal effect from X on Y , potentially through a set of mediators_ **M** _, and given selected conditioned variables_ **S** _, where_ **S** _∩_ **forb** = _∅. ## **2 Optimal adjustment sets**

## **2.1 Information-theoretic characterization**

Figure 1B illustrates two causal effect estimates for a linear Gaussian model consistent with the graph in Fig. 1A. With **Z** = _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 (blue) the error is much larger than with **O** = _Z_ 2 _Z_ 3 (orange) for two reasons: **Z** constrains the residual variance _V ar_ ( _Y |_ **ZS** ) of the effect variable _Y_ less than **O** and, on the other hand, **Z** constrains the residual variance _V ar_ ( _X|_ **ZS** ) of the cause variable _X_ more than **O** . Smaller estimator variance also holds for **O** compared to any other valid set in _Z_ here.

We information-theoretically formalize the resulting intuition to choose an adjustment set **Z** that maximally constrains the effect variable _Y_ and minimally constrains the cause variable _X_ . In terms of CMIs and given selected fixed conditions **S** the quantity to maximize can be stated as follows.

**Definition 1** (Adjustment information) **.** _Consider a causal effect of X on Y for an adjustment set_ **Z** _given a condition set_ **S** _. The_ (conditional) adjustment (set) information _, abbreviated J_ **Z** _, is defined as_

_J_ **Z** is not necessarily positive if the dependence between _X_ and **Z** (given **S** ) is larger than that between **Z** and _Y_ given _X_ **S** . Equation (6) follows from the CMI definition. Fig. 1C illustrates the two CMIs in Eq. (5) in a Venn diagram.

Before discussing the range of estimators for which maximizing the adjustment information _J_ **Z** leads to a minimal asymptotic estimation variance in Sect. 2.2, we characterize graphical optimality in an information-theoretic framework. Our goal is to provide graphical criteria for optimal adjustment sets, i.e., criteria that depend only on the structure of the graph _G_ and not on the distribution.

**Definition 2** (Information-theoretical graphical optimality) **.** _Given Assumptions 1 we say that_ (information-theoretical) graphical optimality holds _if there is a_ **Z** _∈Z such that either there is no other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z or for all other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z and_ all _distributions P consistent with G we have J_ **Z** _≥ J_ **Z** _′._

My main result builds on the following lemma which relates graphical optimality to informationtheoretic inequalities in a necessary and sufficient comparison condition for an optimal set to exist. **Lemma 1** (Necessary and sufficient comparison criterion for existence of an optimal set) **.** _Given Assumptions 1, if and only if there is a_ **Z** _∈Z such that either there is no other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z or for all other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z and all distributions P consistent with G it holds that_

_then graphical optimality holds and_ **Z** _is optimal implying J_ **Z** _≥ J_ **Z** _′._

In SSR20 and HPM19 the corresponding conditional independence statements to the terms (iii) and (iv) in the inequalities (7) are used as a sufficient pairwise comparison criterion. However, Lemma 1 shows that for graphical optimality it is not necessary that terms (iii) and (iv) vanish, they just need to fulfill the inequalities (7) for a _necessary_ and sufficient criterion.

In principle, Lemma 1 can be used to cross-compare all pairs of sets, but firstly, it is difficult to explicitly evaluate (7) for all distributions _P_ consistent with _G_ and, secondly, iterating through all valid adjustment sets is computationally prohibitive even for small graph sizes. As an example, consider a confounding path consisting of 5 nodes. Then this path can be blocked by 2[5] _−_ 1 different subsets. In the main result of this work (Thm. 3) a necessary and sufficient criterion based purely on graphical properties is given.

## **2.3 Definition of O-set**

The optimal adjustment set for the causally sufficient case is simply **P** = _pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) _\_ **forb** and was derived in HPM19 and Rotnitzky and Smucler [2019]. In Section B.2 the derivation is discussed from an information-theoretic perspective. In the case with hidden variables we need to account for bidirected edges “ _↔_ ” which considerably complicate the situation. Then the parents of _Y_ **M** are not sufficient to block all non-causal paths. Further, just like conditioning on parents of _Y_ **M** leads to optimality in the sufficient case since parents constrain information in _Y_ **M** , in the hidden variables case also conditioning on spouses of _Y_ **M** constrains information about _Y_ **M** .

**Example A.** A simple graph (ADMG) to illustrate this is _X→Y ↔Z_ 1 (shown with an additional **S** in Fig. 2A below, or Fig. 4 in SSR20). Here **Z** _[′]_ = _∅_ = **vancs** is a valid set, but it is not optimal. Consider **O** = _Z_ 1, then term (iii) = 0 in the inequalities (7) since **Z** _[′] \_ **O** = _∅_ . Even though not needed to block non-causal paths (there is none), _Z_ 1 still constrains information in _Y_ while being independent of _X_ (hence, term (iv) = 0) which leads to _J_ **O** _> J∅_ according to the inequalities (7).

Not only direct spouses can constrain information in _Y_ as Fig. 2B below illustrates. Since for _W ∈ Y_ **M** the motif “ _W ↔ C_ 1 _←∗C_ 2” (“ _∗_ ” denotes either edge mark) is open, it holds that _I_ ( _C_ 1 _C_ 2; _W_ ) = _I_ ( _C_ 1; _Y_ )+ _I_ ( _C_ 2; _W |C_ 1) _≥ I_ ( _C_ 1; _Y_ ) and we can even further increase the first term in the adjustment

In the following we will abbreviate **O** = **O** ( _X, Y,_ **S** ). Algorithm C.1 states efficient pseudo-code to construct the **O** -set and detect whether a valid adjustment set exists. Since none of the conditions of Def. 3 for adding collider nodes depends on previously added nodes, the algorithm is orderindependent. The statement occurring in lines 11 and 21 (“No valid adjustment set exists.”) is proven in Thm. 1. If the graph is a DAG, then lines 4-22 can be omitted. Numerical experiments in Section 3 will show that further interesting adjustment sets are the _minimized_ **O** -set **O** min, where **O** is minimized such that no subset can be removed without making **O** min invalid, and the _collider-minimized_ **O** -set **O** Cmin where only **CPC** _\_ **P** _⊆_ **O** is minimized such that no collider-subset can be removed without making **O** Cmin invalid. Both adjustment sets can be constructed with Alg. C.2 similar to the efficient algorithms in van der Zander et al. [2019]. Also the minimized sets are order-independent since the nodes are removed only after the for-loops. Based on the idea in **O** Cmin, in the numerical experiments we also consider AdjustXmin, where only Adjust _\ pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) is minimized and _pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) is always included. Finally, we also evaluate Adjustmin where Adjust is fully minimized.

Before discussing the optimality of the **O** -set, we need to assure that it is a valid adjustment set. Similar to the proof given in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for the validity of the **vancs** -set (for the case without **S** ), we can state that the **O** -set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists.

**Theorem 1** (Validity of O-set) **.** _Given Assumptions 1 but_ without _a priori assuming that a valid adjustment set exists (apart from the requirement_ **S** _∩_ **forb** = _∅). If and only if a valid backdoor adjustment set exists, then_ **O** _is a valid adjustment set._

## **2.4 Graphical optimality**

We now move to the question of optimality. It is known that there are graphs where no graphical criterion exists to determine optimality. Examples, discussed later, are the graphs in Figs. 2E,F.

Before stating necessary and sufficient conditions for graphical optimality, I mention that next to the **O** -set defined above and the Adjust set **vancs** [Perkovi´c et al., 2018], I am not aware of any other systematically constructed set that will yield a valid adjustment set for the case with hidden variables. van der Zander et al. [2019] provide algorithms to list all valid adjustment sets, but the question is which of these a user should choose. As mentioned above, Lemma 1 can be used to cross-compare all pairs of sets, but this is not really feasible. Hence, for automated causal effect estimation, rather than the question of whether graphical optimality holds, it is crucial to have a set with better properties than other systematically constructable sets. In the following the **O** -set is illustrated and conditions for graphical optimality are explored. SSR20 provide a sufficient condition for optimality, which states that either all nodes are observed (no bidirected edges exist) or for all observed nodes **V** _⊂_ **vancs** . This is a very strict assumption and not fulfilled for any of the examples (except for Example G) discussed in the following.

**Example B.** Figure 2B depicts a larger example to illustrate the **O** -set **O** = **PCPC** with **P** = _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 _Z_ 3 _Z_ 4 (blue boxes) and **CPC** _\_ **P** = _Z_ 5 _Z_ 6 _Z_ 7 _Z_ 8 (green boxes). We also have a conditioned variable **S** . Among **P** , only _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 are needed to block non-causal paths to _X_ , _Z_ 3 _Z_ 4 are only there to constrain information in _Y_ . Here the same holds for the whole set **CPC** _\_ **P** which was constructed from the paths _Y ↔Z_ 5 _↔Z_ 6 _←Z_ 7 and _Y ↔Z_ 5 _↔Z_ 8 which does not include _Z_ 9 since it is a descendant of _Y_ **M** . Including an independent variable like _Z_ 12 in **O** would not decrease the adjustment information _J_ **O** , but then **O** would not be of minimum cardinality anymore (proven in Cor. B.1). Here, again, the condition of SSR20 does not hold (e.g., _Z_ 5 is not an ancestor of _XY_ **S** ). **O** is optimal here which can be seen as follows: For term (iii) in the inequalities (7) to even be non-zero, we would need a valid **Z** such that **Z** _\_ **O** has a path to _Y_ given **OS** _X_ . But these are all blocked. Note that while _Z_ 10 or _Z_ 9 _∈_ **Z** would open a path to _Y_ , both of these are descendants of **M** or _Y_ and, hence, cannot be in a valid **Z** . For term (iv) to even be non-zero **O** _\_ **Z** would need to have a path to _X_ given **ZS** . But since any valid **Z** has to contain _Z_ 1 and _Z_ 2 (or _Z_ 11), the only nodes in **O** with a path to _X_ are parents of _Y_ **M** and paths from these parents to _X_ all need to be blocked for a valid **Z** . Hence, **O** is optimal here.

## **4 Discussion and Conclusions**

The proposed adjustment information formalizes the common intuition to choose adjustment sets that maximally constrain the effect variable and minimally constrain the cause variable. The main **theoretical contributions** are a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set in the hidden variables case and a definition and algorithm to construct it. To emphasize, graphical optimality implies that the **O** -set is optimal for _any distribution_ consistent with the graph. Note that in cases where graphical optimality does not hold, there will still be distributions for which the **O** -set has maximal adjustment information.

Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has smaller (or equal) asymptotic variance compared to the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not. This makes the **O** -set a natural choice in automated causal inference analyses. **Practical contributions** comprise Python code to construct adjustment sets and check optimality, as well as extensive numerical experiments that demonstrate that the theoretical results also hold for relatively small sample sizes.

The theoretical **optimality results are limited** to estimators for which the asymptotic variance becomes minimal for adjustment sets with maximal adjustment information (relation (8)). This is fulfilled for least-squares estimators, where even the direct relation (9) holds, but it is unclear whether this also holds for more general classes. The numerical results show that the **O** -set or minimized variants thereof often yield smaller variance also in non-optimal settings and beyond that estimator class. I speculate that further theoretical properties of maximizing adjustment information can be shown because relation (9) for _f_ ( _·_ ) = ~~_√_~~ 1 _n[e][H][Y][ |][X]_ **[ZS]** _[−][H][X][|]_ **[ZS]**[seems related to the lower bound of the] estimation variance counterpart to Fano’s inequality (Theorem 8.6.6 in Cover and Thomas [2006]). For estimators sensitive to high-dimensionality one may consider data-driven criteria or penalties to step-wisely minimize the **O** -set. However, estimating, for example, the adjustment information from a potentially small sample size carries considerable errors itself. Another current limitation is that relation (9) only holds for univariate singleton cause variables _X_ . The information-theoretical results, however, also hold for multivariate **X** and preliminary results indicate that, while relation (9) does not hold for multivariate **X** , the less restrictive relation (8) still seems to hold.

The proposed information-theoretic approach can guide **further research** , for example, to theoretically study relations (8),(9) for other estimators and to address other types of graphs as emerge from the output of causal discovery algorithms and the setting where the graph is unknown [Witte et al., 2020, Maathuis et al., 2009, 2010]. At present, the approach only applies to ADMGs and _Maximal Ancestral Graphs_ (MAG) [Richardson and Spirtes, 2002] without selection variables. Last, it remains an open problem to identify optimal adjustment estimands for the hidden variables case based on other criteria such as the front-door formula and Pearl’s general do-calculus [Pearl, 2009].

The results may carry considerable **practical impact** since, surprisingly, among the randomly created configurations more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions indicating that also in many realworld scenarios graphical optimality may hold. progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Review

This paper addresses a well-defined and important open problem in causal inference: deriving a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal backdoor adjustment set in causal graphical models with hidden variables, extending prior work (HPM19, SSR20) that provided only sufficient conditions or handled the causally sufficient case. The contribution appears significant and timely—offering an information-theoretic characterization via "adjustment information," a constructive algorithm, and a claim that the proposed set dominates the Adjust-set of Perković et al. (2018) in all graphs. The scope is clearly situated within the literature, and the inclusion of numerical experiments and released code (`tigramite`) strengthens practical impact. However, several concerns warrant attention. First, a key limitation is candidly acknowledged but under-emphasized in the framing: the translation from adjustment information to asymptotic estimation variance is only verified theoretically for the linear case, which somewhat weakens the practical generality of the "optimality" claim for non-parametric estimators—reviewers will want clearer scoping of what is proven versus conjectured. Second, the abstract's observation that "more than 90%" of random setups satisfy graphical optimality needs careful justification, as the result depends heavily on the random graph generation procedure and may not reflect real-world DAG structures. Third, the writing is dense and assumes substantial familiarity with prior notation (HPM19, SSR20, Perković et al.); a more self-contained articulation of how the information-theoretic framing differs mechanically from the variance-based framing of HPM19 would aid clarity. Minor issues include heavy reliance on the Supplementary Material for proofs and preliminaries, and some awkward first-person phrasing ("My main theoretical contribution") that is atypical for the venue. Overall, this appears to be a strong theoretical contribution with clear novelty, but the gap between the information-theoretic optimality and variance-minimization outside the linear regime is the most important issue to clarify.

comments.title: Collider path motif notation: I(C1C2;W) expansion inconsistency
comments.explanation: The chain rule expansion of I(C1 C2; W) should give I(C1; W) + I(C2; W | C1), not I(C1; Y) + I(C2; W | C1). The first term on the right-hand side should be I(C1; W), since W is the target variable being discussed (the motif is W↔C1←*C2, and W ∈ YM is being constrained). The appearance of 'Y' instead of 'W' in the first term is inconsistent with both the chain rule and with the rest of the sentence. While a reader familiar with W ∈ YM might mentally substitute, as written this is a formula error (likely a typo where W was replaced by Y). This is a localized but real inconsistency in a displayed derivation.

comments.title: Example C: claim that Z_2 has a collider path to X through Z_1 may be mis-stated
comments.quote: But _Z_ 2 cannot be part of any valid **Z** because it has a collider path to _X_ through _Z_ 1 which is always open because it is part of **vancs** .
comments.explanation: Without the figure I cannot fully verify, but the reasoning as stated is puzzling: if Z_1 is conditioned on (being in vancs), then a collider at Z_1 would be opened, not that the path 'is always open because it is part of vancs.' The phrasing conflates 'Z_1 is in vancs' with 'Z_1 is conditioned on in every valid Z.' A valid adjustment set Z need not include Z_1 just because Z_1 ∈ vancs. So the claim that 'Z_2 cannot be part of any valid Z because [a path via Z_1] is always open' is only true if every valid Z conditions on Z_1, which is not explained. This could mislead a careful reader trying to reproduce the logic.

comments.title: Example D: non-trivial inequality claimed without justification
comments.quote: However, it holds that always term (iii) _≤_ (i) because the dependence between _Z_ 1 and _Y_ given _X_ is always smaller than the dependence between _Z_ 2 and _Y_ given _X_ and correspondingly term (iv) _≤_ (ii).
comments.explanation: This is the crux of showing that O is optimal in Example D when the sufficient pairwise criterion of SSR20/HPM19 doesn't apply. The assertion 'dependence between Z_1 and Y given X is always smaller than that between Z_2 and Y given X' is stated as if self-evident, but it is a quantitative claim about CMIs holding for *all* distributions consistent with the graph. Without an explicit argument (e.g., a data-processing-type inequality appealing to the graph structure), a reader cannot verify why this inequality must hold universally. Given this example is specifically showcased as demonstrating the power of the necessary-and-sufficient criterion beyond SSR20/HPM19, the skipped justification is a notable gap.

comments.title: Claim that estimator class extension of Thm. 3 to relation (8) is unproven
comments.quote: at least the main result on graphical optimality in Thm. 3 can also be relaxed to estimators fulfilling the less restrictive relation (8)
comments.explanation: This is a forward/side claim that Thm. 3 extends to estimators satisfying only the weaker relation (8) rather than (9). The paper does not seem to prove this extension explicitly in the passage shown — it's asserted. Given that Lemma 2 is stated using Assumptions 2 (relation 9), extending Thm. 3 to relation (8) would require additional argument. This is at least a minor overclaim without justification shown here, though the paper acknowledges the question is left open.

comments.title: Claim that 'O-set is optimal for any distribution' when graphical optimality holds vs. requiring Assumption 2
comments.quote: To emphasize, graphical optimality implies that the **O** -set is optimal for _any distribution_ consistent with the graph.
comments.explanation: On first read this sounds like a strong unqualified claim. Checking against the formal setup: graphical optimality (Def. 2) is defined in terms of J_Z, i.e., adjustment information. Optimality in the sense of minimum asymptotic variance requires Assumptions 2 (the estimator class relation (9)). The Discussion does note this limitation later ('optimality results are limited to estimators for which the asymptotic variance becomes minimal...'), so a careful reader can resolve the qualifier. However, the sentence in isolation overstates: 'O-set is optimal' without qualification could be read as variance-optimal for any estimator, when really it is only information-theoretically optimal (or variance-optimal for estimators satisfying (9)).

comments.title: Example B: inequality in collider-path information claim
comments.explanation: This appears in Section 2.3 (not the passage proper, but context). The chain rule would give I(C_1 C_2; W) = I(C_1; W) + I(C_2; W | C_1), not I(C_1; Y). The authors mix W and Y within a single equation. Since W is said to be in YM, perhaps they intend W=Y in this instance, but then the equation should read I(C_1 C_2; Y) = I(C_1; Y) + I(C_2; Y | C_1). As written, it's a notational/indexing inconsistency that could confuse readers trying to verify the claim.

comments.title: Example C: claim that Z_2 has a collider path to X through Z_1 may be mis-stated
comments.quote: But _Z_ 2 cannot be part of any valid **Z** because it has a collider path to _X_ through _Z_ 1 which is always open because it is part of **vancs** .
comments.explanation: Without the figure I cannot fully verify, but the reasoning as stated is puzzling: if Z_1 is conditioned on (being in vancs), then a collider at Z_1 would be opened, not that the path 'is always open because it is part of vancs.' The phrasing conflates 'Z_1 is in vancs' with 'Z_1 is conditioned on in every valid Z.' A valid adjustment set Z need not include Z_1 just because Z_1 ∈ vancs. So the claim that 'Z_2 cannot be part of any valid Z because [a path via Z_1] is always open' is only true if every valid Z conditions on Z_1, which is not explained. This could mislead a careful reader trying to reproduce the logic.

comments.title: Example D: non-trivial inequality claimed without justification
comments.quote: However, it holds that always term (iii) _≤_ (i) because the dependence between _Z_ 1 and _Y_ given _X_ is always smaller than the dependence between _Z_ 2 and _Y_ given _X_ and correspondingly term (iv) _≤_ (ii).
comments.explanation: This is the crux of showing that O is optimal in Example D when the sufficient pairwise criterion of SSR20/HPM19 doesn't apply. The assertion 'dependence between Z_1 and Y given X is always smaller than that between Z_2 and Y given X' is stated as if self-evident, but it is a quantitative claim about CMIs holding for *all* distributions consistent with the graph. Without an explicit argument (e.g., a data-processing-type inequality appealing to the graph structure), a reader cannot verify why this inequality must hold universally. Given this example is specifically showcased as demonstrating the power of the necessary-and-sufficient criterion beyond SSR20/HPM19, the skipped justification is a notable gap.

comments.title: Example E derivation of J_{Z_1 Z_2} uses inconsistent conditioning
comments.explanation: The author writes J_{Z_1 Z_2} = J_∅ + I_{Z_2;Y|XZ_1} − I_{X;Z_1}. Let me check: J_Z = I_{Z;Y|XS} − I_{X;Z|S}. With S=∅, J_{Z_1 Z_2} = I_{Z_1 Z_2;Y|X} − I_{X;Z_1 Z_2}. By chain rule I_{Z_1 Z_2;Y|X} = I_{Z_1;Y|X} + I_{Z_2;Y|XZ_1}, and I_{X;Z_1 Z_2} = I_{X;Z_1} + I_{X;Z_2|Z_1}. Since Z_1 ⊥ Y | X (stated) and X ⊥ Z_2 | Z_1 (stated), this gives J_{Z_1 Z_2} = 0 + I_{Z_2;Y|XZ_1} − I_{X;Z_1} − 0. Also J_∅ = I_{∅;Y|X} − I_{X;∅} = 0. So the formula J_{Z_1 Z_2} = J_∅ + I_{Z_2;Y|XZ_1} − I_{X;Z_1} is actually correct given J_∅ = 0. However, the identity in the more general sense should have I_{X;Z_1|∅} which is just I_{X;Z_1}; the term I_{Z_2;Y|XZ_1} is correct. On careful check this is consistent, though the skipping of J_∅=0 might be confusing. Not a real error.

comments.title: Theorem 3 condition (I) wording: 'inside C' may be ambiguous
comments.quote: For_ all _N ∈_ **N** _and all its collider paths i to W ∈ Y_ **M** _that are inside_ **C**
comments.explanation: The phrase 'collider paths i to W ∈ YM that are inside C' is ambiguous. From Def. 3, a collider path of W is W↔C_1↔...↔C_k. Here we have a path from N (an N-node, not in O) to W passing through colliders — the intermediate colliders π_i^N should be in C (valid collider nodes of the O-set). The condition seems to say the interior collider nodes lie inside C. This is a terminology concern: 'inside C' to refer to the set **C** defined in Def. 4 vs. the path nodes being colliders. A careful reader may be briefly confused whether 'C' denotes the set **C** from Def. 4 or just 'colliders'. Given the paper earlier writes C ∈ **C** explicitly in Cond. (II), the intent here is the set **C**. This is a minor ambiguity, probably resolvable from context.

comments.title: Possible OCR/notation inconsistency in Example F analysis
comments.explanation: The expression **O**_{π_i^N} = O(X, Y, S' = Z_2) = Z_1 Z_2 claims that the O-set computed with S' = {Z_2} (i.e., conditioning on the N-node) equals {Z_1, Z_2}. But the earlier definition states S' = S ∪ {N} ∪ π_i^N. For Example F, S = ∅, N = Z_2, and the path from N=Z_2 to X is a non-collider path (stated: 'Z_2 is an N-node with a non-collider path to X'). Wait — Theorem 3's construction uses collider paths N↔...↔C↔...↔W to Y/M, not paths to X. If π_i^N is empty (direct link N↔W), then S' = {N} = {Z_2}. So S' = Z_2 makes sense. The computation O(X,Y,S'={Z_2}) = Z_1 Z_2 is plausible since adding Z_2 to S makes Z_1 an ancestor of the conditioned set. This checks out, though it is terse.

comments.title: Claim about estimator class verification is repeated but unverified beyond Gaussian OLS
comments.quote: at least the main result on graphical optimality in Thm. 3 can also be relaxed to estimators fulfilling the less restrictive relation (8)
comments.explanation: This is a forward/side claim that Thm. 3 extends to estimators satisfying only the weaker relation (8) rather than (9). The paper does not seem to prove this extension explicitly in the passage shown — it's asserted. Given that Lemma 2 is stated using Assumptions 2 (relation 9), extending Thm. 3 to relation (8) would require additional argument. This is at least a minor overclaim without justification shown here, though the paper acknowledges the question is left open. Not major.

comments.title: Direction of inequality reversed in Discussion claim about O-set vs Adjust-set
comments.quote: Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has smaller (or equal) asymptotic variance compared to the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not.
comments.explanation: Initially this reads fine as a natural claim. But checking against the abstract and Theorem 2, the paper consistently states that J_O ≥ J_vancs (O-set has higher or equal adjustment information). Via Lemma 2, higher adjustment information corresponds to smaller or equal asymptotic variance, so the direction here ('smaller or equal asymptotic variance') is consistent with the earlier claims. On reflection this is correct — I initially worried it might contradict the abstract's 'higher (or equal) adjustment information' phrasing but the two are equivalent via Lemma 2. No issue.

comments.title: Claim that 'O-set is optimal for any distribution' when graphical optimality holds vs. requiring Assumption 2
comments.quote: To emphasize, graphical optimality implies that the **O** -set is optimal for _any distribution_ consistent with the graph.
comments.explanation: On first read this sounds like a strong unqualified claim. Checking against the formal setup: graphical optimality (Def. 2) is defined in terms of J_Z, i.e., adjustment information. Optimality in the sense of minimum asymptotic variance requires Assumptions 2 (the estimator class relation (9)). The Discussion does note this limitation later ('optimality results are limited to estimators for which the asymptotic variance becomes minimal...'), so a careful reader can resolve the qualifier. However, the sentence in isolation overstates: 'O-set is optimal' without qualification could be read as variance-optimal for any estimator, when really it is only information-theoretically optimal (or variance-optimal for estimators satisfying (9)). Mild overclaim that is partially clarified in the next paragraph.

comments.title: Percentage of non-optimal configurations stated as 7% vs. abstract's >90% optimal
comments.quote: Secondly, in non-optimal settings (only 7% of configurations)
comments.explanation: The abstract says 'more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions', which is consistent with non-optimal being up to ~10%. Stating 7% here is within that bound and not contradictory — 'more than 90%' is consistent with '93% optimal / 7% non-optimal'. No actual inconsistency.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and technically challenging open problem—optimal adjustment in ADMGs with hidden variables—and the claimed contribution, a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion plus constructive algorithm, is potentially substantial and of clear interest to the causal inference community. The introduction does a good job positioning the work relative to Henckel et al., Perković et al., and Smucler et al., and it clearly states both the theoretical and empirical contributions. However, the presentation is quite dense and assumes considerable prior familiarity with adjustment theory, ADMGs, asymptotic efficiency, and information-theoretic characterizations; as a result, the core intuition behind “adjustment information” and why it resolves the hidden-variable obstruction is not yet sufficiently transparent for a broad NeurIPS audience. The paper would benefit from an early, simple motivating example showing when no graphically optimal set exists, how the proposed criterion detects this, and how the constructed set improves on existing candidates. A major issue to clarify is the scope of the optimality claim: the abstract and introduction strongly emphasize minimal asymptotic variance, but later qualify this as applying only to estimator classes satisfying a particular information-theoretic variance relation, apparently verified theoretically only in the linear case. This limitation should be foregrounded more carefully to avoid overstating the generality of the results. Similarly, claims such as “optimal set is valid iff a valid adjustment set exists” and “higher adjustment information than Adjust-set for any graph” are potentially very strong and need precise conditions stated early. Overall, the work appears ambitious, original, and potentially impactful, but its quality will depend heavily on the rigor and accessibility of the formal criterion, proofs, and algorithmic construction; the main risks are overclaiming beyond the verified estimator class and insufficient intuitive exposition of the central graphical and information-theoretic ideas.

comments.title: Ambiguity between distribution-specific and graphical optimality
comments.quote: For the case with hidden variables there can be settings where no optimal set exists and currently only a sufficient graphical optimality criterion of limited applicability has been derived.
comments.explanation: For a fixed graph and fixed data-generating distribution, the set of candidate adjustment sets is finite, so if at least one valid adjustment set exists then a minimum-variance adjustment set should exist, possibly with ties. The intended meaning appears to be that no single graphically determined adjustment set is uniformly optimal over all distributions compatible with the graph; the variance-minimizing set may depend on coefficients, noise terms, or other distributional details. Similar wording later says that “no graphical criterion exists to determine optimality,” which is also stronger than necessary given that the paper aims to characterize when graphical optimality holds. A more precise formulation would be that no graphically optimal adjustment set exists in those settings, or that the graph alone cannot select a uniformly optimal set.

comments.title: Valid adjustment does not by itself make estimators unbiased
comments.quote: Estimators of causal effects based on such a valid adjustment set as a covariate are then unbiased, but for different adjustment sets the estimation variance may strongly vary.
comments.explanation: A valid adjustment set identifies the causal estimand, but it does not by itself guarantee finite-sample unbiasedness for an arbitrary estimator. Regression adjustment, nonparametric plug-in estimators, and related methods usually require additional assumptions such as correct model specification, positivity, and regularity, and are often only consistent or asymptotically unbiased. Later parts of the paper are more careful about consistency under valid adjustment and correct functional model specification, so this concern is localized to the wording of this sentence.

comments.title: “Minimum cardinality” is not equivalent to no removable node
comments.quote: The derived optimal adjustment set also has the property of minimum cardinality, i.e., no node can be removed without sacrificing optimality.
comments.explanation: The explanation after “i.e.” describes inclusion-minimality: no single element can be removed while preserving optimality. That is not the same as minimum cardinality, which would mean having the smallest size among all optimal adjustment sets. Inclusion-minimal optimal sets can have different cardinalities. If the theorem proves global minimum cardinality, the gloss is too weak; if it proves only that no variable can be removed, then “minimum cardinality” overstates the result and should be replaced by “inclusion-minimal” or “minimal with respect to deletion.”

comments.title: Scope of the estimator-class verification is stated too broadly as “linear case”
comments.quote: The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation that, at present, I could only verify theoretically for the linear case.
comments.explanation: The later entropy-based argument appears to be derived specifically for Gaussian distributions, where conditional entropies are functions of conditional variances. Standard linear SEM or OLS asymptotic variance results do not generally imply the same entropy relation outside Gaussian or otherwise specially structured distributions, because entropy is not determined by variance in non-Gaussian settings. If the verification is only for OLS under linear Gaussian models, then “linear case” is broader than justified and should be narrowed to “linear Gaussian case” or otherwise specify the estimator and distributional assumptions.

comments.title: Random-setup frequency is weak evidence for real-world prevalence
comments.quote: Surprisingly, among the randomly created setups more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions indicating that also in many real-world scenarios graphical optimality may hold.
comments.explanation: The empirical observation is appropriately framed as coming from randomly created setups, and “may” hedges the conclusion. Still, the inference to “many real-world scenarios” depends on whether the random graph model, parameterization, sparsity, hidden-variable structure, and selection mechanism are representative of real causal graphs. The safer conclusion is that graphical optimality is common under the authors’ simulation design and worth checking in applications, not that the simulations indicate real-world prevalence.

comments.title: Validity definition omits the conditioning set S used throughout the problem
comments.quote: A (possibly empty) set of adjustment variables **Z** for the total causal effect of _X_ on _Y_ in an ADMG is called _valid_ relative to ( _X, Y_ ) if the interventional distribution for setting _do_ ( _X_ = **x** ) factorizes as _p_ ( _Y |do_ ( _X_ = **x** )) =  _p_ ( _Y |_ **x** _,_ **z** ) _p_ ( **z** ) _d_ **z** for non-empty **Z** and as _p_ ( _Y |do_ ( _X_ = **x** )) = _p_ ( _Y |_ **x** ) for empty **Z** .
comments.explanation: The displayed formula is the standard unconditional adjustment formula, but the paper’s target is the conditional causal effect given selected variables **S** = **s**, and subsequent notation and examples condition on **Z** together with **S**. For a conditional effect, one would expect a formula such as p(Y | do(x), S=s) = ∫ p(Y | x, z, s) p(z | s) dz, with the graphical criterion involving the combined conditioning set. The later definition of **vancs** seems to incorporate **S**, so the intended notion is likely validity given **S**. As written, however, the formal definition and empty-set case are unconditional, which can change both the adjustment formula and the graphical blocking condition.

comments.title: Asymptotic variance is written as an unscaled finite-sample squared error
comments.quote: More formally, the task is, given a graph _G_ and ( _X, Y,_ **S** ), to chose a valid optimal set **Z** optimal _∈Z_ such that the causal effect estimator’s asymptotic variance Var(∆[] _yxx′|_ **s** _._ **z** ) = _E_ [(∆ _yxx′|_ **s** _−_ ∆[] _yxx′|_ **s** _._ **z** )[2] ] is minimal:
comments.explanation: The text equates asymptotic variance with an ordinary mean squared error E[(Δ − Δ̂)^2], without the usual scaling or limiting operation. For a consistent estimator, this unscaled quantity typically converges to zero for all valid adjustment sets. Asymptotic variance usually refers to Var(√n(Δ̂ − Δ)) or to a limit such as lim n Var(Δ̂). Since variance optimality is central to the paper, this formal statement should use the appropriate asymptotic object.

comments.title: Ambiguous count of blocking subsets for a 5-node confounding path
comments.quote: As an example, consider a confounding path consisting of 5 nodes. Then this path can be blocked by 2[5] _−_ 1 different subsets.
comments.explanation: If the path has five eligible non-collider nodes between X and Y, then any nonempty subset of those five nodes would block the path, giving 2^5 − 1 subsets. But “a confounding path consisting of 5 nodes” is naturally read as a path with five total vertices, including endpoints X and Y, leaving only three internal nodes available for adjustment and at most 2^3 − 1 blocking subsets. The statement also assumes all candidate nodes are non-colliders and eligible for adjustment. This does not affect the general point about combinatorial growth, but the example is graph-theoretically ambiguous.

comments.title: Collider motif described as open without specifying conditioning
comments.quote: Since for _W ∈ Y_ **M** the motif “ _W ↔ C_ 1 _←∗C_ 2” (“ _∗_ ” denotes either edge mark) is open, it holds that _I_ ( _C_ 1 _C_ 2; _W_ ) = _I_ ( _C_ 1; _Y_ )+ _I_ ( _C_ 2; _W |C_ 1) _≥ I_ ( _C_ 1; _Y_ )
comments.explanation: The intended information-theoretic point follows from the chain rule and nonnegativity of conditional mutual information. However, the graphical statement that the motif W ↔ C1 ←∗ C2 “is open” is problematic under standard m-separation: C1 is a collider on the path W–C1–C2, so the path is blocked unless C1 or a descendant of C1 is conditioned on. The displayed term I(C2; W | C1) does condition on C1, so the intended statement may be that the path becomes open after conditioning on C1. As written, it says the motif itself is open, which conflicts with the usual collider convention.

comments.title: O-set formula may include the intervention variable unless an exclusion convention is explicit
comments.quote: The optimal adjustment set for the causally sufficient case is simply **P** = _pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) _\_ **forb** and was derived in HPM19 and Rotnitzky and Smucler [2019].
comments.explanation: The formula matches the known O-set intuition: adjust for parents of the outcome and mediators, excluding forbidden nodes. But under ordinary parent-set notation, pa(Y ∪ M) can include X whenever X → Y or X → M. Adjustment sets normally exclude the intervention variable, and the regression formulation treats X separately from Z. Thus the formula is unambiguous only if **forb** includes X, if pa(YM) is being used with an implicit exclusion of X, or if the intended expression also subtracts X. The surrounding notation does not by itself make that exclusion clear.

comments.title: Minimized adjustment sets may be non-unique and the order-independence claim is not justified
comments.quote: Numerical experiments in Section 3 will show that further interesting adjustment sets are the _minimized_ **O** -set **O** min, where **O** is minimized such that no subset can be removed without making **O** min invalid, and the _collider-minimized_ **O** -set **O** Cmin where only **CPC** _\_ **P** _⊆_ **O** is minimized such that no collider-subset can be removed without making **O** Cmin invalid. Both adjustment sets can be constructed with Alg. C.2 similar to the efficient algorithms in van der Zander et al. [2019]. Also the minimized sets are order-independent since the nodes are removed only after the for-loops.
comments.explanation: Minimal valid adjustment subsets are generally not unique: two covariates can be mutually redundant, so removing either one alone preserves validity while removing both destroys validity. A sequential greedy minimization can therefore depend on removal order, while marking all individually removable nodes and deleting them after a loop can remove too much and make the result invalid. The statement that order-independence follows because nodes are removed after the for-loops is not sufficient without a uniqueness proof or a more precise algorithmic specification.

comments.title: Stated SSR20 sufficient condition appears set-theoretically ambiguous
comments.quote: SSR20 provide a sufficient condition for optimality, which states that either all nodes are observed (no bidirected edges exist) or for all observed nodes **V** _⊂_ **vancs**.
comments.explanation: Earlier, **vancs** is defined as an(XY**S**) \ **forb**, which is itself a subset of the observed node set **V**. Thus **V** ⊂ **vancs** is either impossible if ⊂ is strict, or collapses to **V** = **vancs** if it is used non-strictly. The phrase “for all observed nodes **V**” also mixes node-level and set-level language. The intended condition may be that every observed node belongs to **vancs**, or it may refer to a different set from SSR20, but as written the condition is hard to check.

comments.title: O-set versus Adjust-set comparison is overbroad and missing qualifiers
comments.quote: Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has smaller (or equal) asymptotic variance compared to the Adjust-set proposed in Perković et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not.
comments.explanation: The surrounding context suggests that the intended object is the constructed **O**-set, not an “optimal set” in the graphically optimal sense, because the sentence explicitly includes cases where graphical optimality does not hold. The variance claim also needs the estimator-class qualifier: the theorem appears to establish an information-theoretic comparison between the **O**-set and the Adjust-set, while translating that comparison into smaller or equal asymptotic variance requires estimators satisfying the stated relation. Finally, “for any graph” should be read under the paper’s assumptions, especially existence of a valid adjustment set. A more precise statement would say that, for graphs satisfying the assumptions, the **O**-set is valid iff a valid adjustment set exists and has at least as much adjustment information as Adjust; for estimators satisfying the required relation, this implies no larger asymptotic variance.

comments.title: Numerical experiments are described as demonstrating finite-sample validity too broadly
comments.quote: Practical contributions comprise Python code to construct adjustment sets and check optimality, as well as extensive numerical experiments that demonstrate that the theoretical results also hold for relatively small sample sizes.
comments.explanation: The experiments can illustrate or corroborate that the predicted rankings often persist at small sample sizes in the studied configurations. But the theoretical results are graphical and asymptotic statements under specified assumptions, while finite-sample performance depends on the data-generating process, estimator implementation, tuning choices, sample size, and adjustment-set size. Simulations do not demonstrate that the theoretical results generally “hold” at small samples; they provide empirical support in the simulated settings.

comments.title: Ambiguity between distribution-specific and graphical optimality
comments.quote: For the case with hidden variables there can be settings where no optimal set exists and currently only a sufficient graphical optimality criterion of limited applicability has been derived.
comments.explanation: My initial concern was that, for a fixed graph and a fixed data-generating distribution, the set of candidate adjustment sets is finite, so if at least one valid adjustment set exists then an adjustment set with minimum asymptotic variance should exist, possibly with ties. Reading further, the intended meaning appears to be that no single graphically determined adjustment set is uniformly optimal over all distributions compatible with the graph; the introduction later clarifies this by saying that the optimal adjustment set may depend on coefficients, noise terms, or the distribution rather than just the graph. That resolves the mathematical point, but the quoted abstract sentence itself says simply that “no optimal set exists,” which could mislead readers into thinking there is no distribution-specific minimizer. A more precise statement would be that no graphically optimal adjustment set exists in those settings.

comments.title: Valid adjustment does not by itself make estimators unbiased
comments.quote: Estimators of causal effects based on such a valid adjustment set as a covariate are then unbiased, but for different adjustment sets the estimation variance may strongly vary.
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear and standard: a valid adjustment set identifies the causal estimand, and different valid sets can yield estimators with different variances. However, validity of an adjustment set is an identification property, not by itself a finite-sample unbiasedness guarantee for an arbitrary estimator. Regression adjustment, nonparametric plug-in estimators, and other estimators are typically consistent or asymptotically unbiased only under additional conditions such as correct model specification, positivity, and regularity assumptions. The later context is more careful, referring to consistency under a valid adjustment set and correct functional model specification. Thus the concern is localized to this prose sentence, but “unbiased” is technically stronger than what follows from graphical validity alone.

comments.title: “Minimum cardinality” is not equivalent to no removable node
comments.quote: The derived optimal adjustment set also has the property of minimum cardinality, i.e., no node can be removed without sacrificing optimality.
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to convey that the constructed optimal set has no superfluous variables, which is a useful property. But the phrase after “i.e.” describes inclusion-minimality among optimal sets: no single element can be removed while preserving optimality. That is not the same as minimum cardinality, which would mean having the smallest number of variables among all optimal adjustment sets. In set optimization these are distinct notions; there can be several inclusion-minimal optimal sets of different sizes. If the theorem really proves global minimum cardinality, the explanatory gloss is too weak and potentially misleading. If it only proves that no variable can be removed, then “minimum cardinality” overstates the result and should be replaced by “inclusion-minimal” or “minimal with respect to deletion.”

comments.title: Scope of the estimator-class verification is stated too broadly as “linear case”
comments.quote: The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation that, at present, I could only verify theoretically for the linear case.
comments.explanation: The qualification that the variance result only applies to estimators satisfying a specific information-theoretic relation is helpful. My concern is with the phrase “the linear case.” In the later estimator section, the displayed entropy argument is explicitly derived for Gaussian distributions, where conditional entropies are functions of conditional variances. Standard linear SEM or OLS asymptotic variance results do not generally imply the same entropy relation outside Gaussian or otherwise specially structured distributions, because entropy is not determined by variance in non-Gaussian settings. If the paper only verifies the required relation for OLS under Gaussian linear models, then “linear case” is broader than justified and may be read as covering arbitrary linear models with non-Gaussian errors. The statement would be safer if it said “linear Gaussian case” or specified exactly which linear estimator/distributional assumptions are covered.

comments.title: Random-setup frequency is weak evidence for real-world prevalence
comments.quote: Surprisingly, among the randomly created setups more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions indicating that also in many real-world scenarios graphical optimality may hold.
comments.explanation: The empirical observation itself is clearly framed as coming from randomly created setups, and the word “may” appropriately hedges the conclusion. Still, the inference from randomly generated graphs/setups to “many real-world scenarios” depends heavily on the random graph model, parameterization, sparsity, hidden-variable structure, and selection mechanism used in the experiments. Without specifying that those random setups are representative of real causal graphs in the domains of interest, the sentence risks overstating what the simulations show. The safer interpretation is that graphical optimality is not rare under the authors’ simulation design, which motivates checking it in applications; it does not by itself indicate prevalence in real-world scenarios.

comments.title: Validity definition omits the conditioning set S used throughout the problem
comments.quote: A (possibly empty) set of adjustment variables **Z** for the total causal effect of _X_ on _Y_ in an ADMG is called _valid_ relative to ( _X, Y_ ) if the interventional distribution for setting _do_ ( _X_ = **x** ) [Pearl, 2009] factorizes as _p_ ( _Y |do_ ( _X_ = **x** )) =  _p_ ( _Y |_ **x** _,_ **z** ) _p_ ( **z** ) _d_ **z** for non-empty **Z** and as _p_ ( _Y |do_ ( _X_ = **x** )) = _p_ ( _Y |_ **x** ) for empty **Z** .
comments.explanation: I initially read this as the standard unconditional adjustment formula, and in that setting it is fine. However, the paper’s stated target immediately afterward is the conditional causal effect given selected variables **S** = **s**, and subsequent notation and examples use regressions/estimators conditioned on **ZS**. For a conditional effect, a reader would normally expect a formula of the form \(p(Y\mid do(x),S=s)=\int p(Y\mid x,z,s)p(z\mid s)dz\) for suitable pre-treatment/conditioned variables, and the graphical blocking condition should involve paths blocked by the combined conditioning set, typically **Z** together with **S**. The later definition of **vancs** apparently incorporates **S**, so the authors likely intend validity “given **S**.” But as written, the formal definition of validity and the empty-set case are unconditional, which could misclassify adjustment sets in cases where **S** blocks or opens paths. This is not merely an OCR symbol issue; the omitted conditioning changes the adjustment formula and graphical criterion.

comments.title: Asymptotic variance is written as an unscaled finite-sample squared error
comments.quote: More formally, the task is, given a graph _G_ and ( _X, Y,_ **S** ), to chose a valid optimal set **Z** optimal _∈Z_ such that the causal effect estimator’s asymptotic variance Var(∆[] _yxx′|_ **s** _._ **z** ) = _E_ [(∆ _yxx′|_ **s** _−_ ∆[] _yxx′|_ **s** _._ **z** )[2] ] is minimal:
comments.explanation: The surrounding discussion makes clear that the authors care about asymptotic estimation variance, which is the right object for the cited optimal-adjustment literature. But the displayed characterization equates it with an ordinary mean squared error, \(E[(\Delta-\hat\Delta)^2]\), without the usual asymptotic scaling or limiting operation. For a consistent estimator this unscaled quantity typically converges to zero for every valid adjustment set, whereas asymptotic variance conventionally refers to the variance of \(\sqrt{n}(\hat\Delta-\Delta)\), or equivalently a limit such as \(\lim_{n\to\infty} n\,\mathrm{Var}(\hat\Delta)\). Later sections may use the intended asymptotic object, but this statement is formally inaccurate and could confuse the optimization criterion, especially because variance optimality is a central claim of the paper.

comments.title: Ambiguous count of blocking subsets for a 5-node confounding path
comments.quote: As an example, consider a confounding path consisting of 5 nodes. Then this path can be blocked by 2[5] _−_ 1 different subsets.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent here: they are giving a quick combinatorial illustration of why enumerating all valid adjustment sets is infeasible. If the path has five eligible non-collider nodes between X and Y, then indeed any nonempty subset of those five nodes would block that path, giving 2^5 - 1 subsets. However, the phrase “a confounding path consisting of 5 nodes” would normally be read as a path with five total vertices, including the endpoints X and Y. Under that standard reading there are only three internal nodes available for adjustment, giving at most 2^3 - 1 blocking subsets, and endpoints are not adjustment variables. Also, the statement assumes all five candidate nodes are non-colliders and eligible for adjustment. This does not affect the main argument about combinatorial growth, but the numerical example is ambiguous enough that a careful reader could misread it as a literal graph-theoretic count.

comments.title: Collider motif described as open without specifying conditioning
comments.quote: Since for _W ∈ Y_ **M** the motif “ _W ↔ C_ 1 _←∗C_ 2” (“ _∗_ ” denotes either edge mark) is open, it holds that _I_ ( _C_ 1 _C_ 2; _W_ ) = _I_ ( _C_ 1; _Y_ )+ _I_ ( _C_ 2; _W |C_ 1) _≥ I_ ( _C_ 1; _Y_ )
comments.explanation: The intended information-theoretic point is clear: by the chain rule, adding C2 after already conditioning on C1 can only increase the information about W, since conditional mutual information is nonnegative. I also assume the occurrences of Y in I(C1;Y) are likely OCR or notation slips for W, because the surrounding sentence is about arbitrary W ∈ Y∪M and the chain rule would then read I(C1C2;W)=I(C1;W)+I(C2;W|C1). What remains problematic is the graphical statement that the motif W↔C1←∗C2 “is open.” Under standard m-separation in ADMGs, C1 is a collider on the path W–C1–C2, so the path from W to C2 is blocked unless C1 or a descendant of C1 is conditioned on. The displayed CMI term I(C2;W|C1) does condition on C1, so the intended statement may be that the path becomes open after C1 has been included. But as written, it says the motif itself is open, which is the opposite of the usual collider convention and could confuse the rationale for valid collider paths.

comments.title: O-set formula may include the intervention variable unless an exclusion convention is made explicit
comments.quote: The optimal adjustment set for the causally sufficient case is simply **P** = _pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) _\_ **forb** and was derived in HPM19 and Rotnitzky and Smucler [2019].
comments.explanation: The high-level claim matches the known O-set intuition: in the causally sufficient case, one adjusts for parents of the outcome and mediators, excluding forbidden nodes. However, under ordinary parent-set notation, pa(Y∪M) can include X whenever there is a direct edge X→Y or X→M, which is precisely allowed in the paper’s setting. Adjustment sets are normally subsets of pre-treatment covariates excluding the intervention variable, and the regression formulation already includes X separately from Z. Thus P = pa(YM)\forb is only correct if either forb has been defined to include X, or pa(YM) is being used with an implicit exclusion of X, or the intended expression also subtracts X. I checked the surrounding context: earlier kinship notation says pa(X) excludes X itself, but that does not by itself exclude X as a parent of Y or M. This may be resolved by a fuller definition of forb or by a convention elsewhere, but in the passage as written the formula is ambiguous and could otherwise produce an invalid adjustment set.

comments.title: Minimized adjustment sets may be non-unique and the order-independence claim is not justified
comments.quote: Numerical experiments in Section 3 will show that further interesting adjustment sets are the _minimized_ **O** -set **O** min, where **O** is minimized such that no subset can be removed without making **O** min invalid, and the _collider-minimized_ **O** -set **O** Cmin where only **CPC** _\_ **P** _⊆_ **O** is minimized such that no collider-subset can be removed without making **O** Cmin invalid. Both adjustment sets can be constructed with Alg. C.2 similar to the efficient algorithms in van der Zander et al. [2019]. Also the minimized sets are order-independent since the nodes are removed only after the for-loops.
comments.explanation: The intent is clear: the authors want smaller valid variants of the O-set for empirical comparison, analogous to minimal adjustment sets. However, I do not see from this passage why these minimized sets are well-defined or order-independent. Minimal valid adjustment subsets are generally not unique: two covariates can be mutually redundant, so removing either one alone preserves validity, while removing both destroys validity. In such a case, a sequential greedy minimization depends on the order of removal, whereas marking all individually removable nodes and removing them only after a loop can remove too much and make the resulting set invalid. Thus the statement that order-independence follows simply because nodes are removed after the for-loops is not sufficient. This may be resolved in Algorithm C.2, but as stated the definition does not specify tie-breaking, whether all removable singletons or arbitrary subsets are tested, or why simultaneous removals preserve validity. Since these sets are later used in numerical comparisons, the construction needs either a uniqueness proof or a more precise algorithmic specification.

comments.title: Stated SSR20 sufficient condition appears set-theoretically ambiguous
comments.quote: SSR20 provide a sufficient condition for optimality, which states that either all nodes are observed (no bidirected edges exist) or for all observed nodes **V** _⊂_ **vancs**.
comments.explanation: I understand this sentence as a high-level comparison to SSR20 rather than a central derivation. Still, the notation is confusing in light of the earlier definition **vancs** = an(XY**S**) \ **forb**, which is itself a subset of the observed node set **V**. Thus the displayed relation **V** ⊂ **vancs** is either impossible if ⊂ is meant strictly, or collapses to **V** = **vancs** if ⊂ is used non-strictly. The phrase “for all observed nodes **V**” also mixes node-level and set-level language. Perhaps the intended condition is that every observed node belongs to **vancs**, or perhaps a different set from SSR20 is meant. As written, a reader cannot reliably check the claim that the condition fails for the examples except Example G.

comments.title: “No graphical criterion exists” conflicts with the paper’s own graphical optimality objective
comments.quote: We now move to the question of optimality. It is known that there are graphs where no graphical criterion exists to determine optimality. Examples, discussed later, are the graphs in Figs. 2E,F.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intended meaning seems to be that, for some graphs, no single adjustment set is graphically optimal uniformly over all distributions compatible with the graph; the variance-minimizing set can depend on coefficients or distributional details. That is consistent with the introduction and with the later goal of giving conditions for when graphical optimality holds. But the wording “no graphical criterion exists to determine optimality” is stronger and potentially contradictory, because the paper itself claims to provide necessary and sufficient graphical conditions for the existence of an optimal adjustment set. A more precise formulation would be that graphical optimality fails for those graphs, or that the graph alone cannot select a uniformly optimal set.

comments.title: “Any graph” overstates the scope of the O-set versus Adjust-set comparison
comments.quote: the adjustment informations follow _J_ **O** _≥ J_ **vancs** for _any_ graph (whether graphical optimality holds or not).
comments.explanation: The intended contrast is useful: even when no graphically optimal adjustment set exists, the constructed **O**-set still dominates the Adjust-set in adjustment information. The formal theorem immediately below partially resolves the concern by adding “Given Assumptions 1,” and Assumptions 1 normally include the existence of at least one valid adjustment set. But the prose phrase “for any graph” could be read more broadly, including graphs where no valid adjustment set exists. In those cases **vancs** may not be a valid estimator-adjustment set, Algorithm C.1 may return “No valid adjustment set exists,” and the practical meaning of comparing adjustment informations is less clear. The statement would be safer as “for any graph satisfying Assumptions 1” or “for any graph with a valid adjustment set, regardless of whether graphical optimality holds.”

comments.title: “Optimal set” is used where the O-set seems intended
comments.quote: Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has smaller (or equal) asymptotic variance compared to the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a statement about the graphically optimal adjustment set. But the end of the sentence explicitly includes cases where graphical optimality does not hold, and in those cases an optimal set in the sense of Definition 2 need not exist. From the surrounding context and the earlier summary of the results, the intended object appears to be the constructed **O**-set: it is valid iff a valid adjustment set exists, and it has adjustment information at least as large as Adjust. Thus the mathematical content is likely correct if “the optimal set” is read as “the **O**-set” or “the proposed candidate optimal set.” As written, however, it conflicts with the paper’s own definition of graphical optimality and could mislead readers into thinking an optimal set exists even in non-optimal configurations.

comments.title: Variance dominance over Adjust is missing the estimator-class qualifier
comments.quote: Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has smaller (or equal) asymptotic variance compared to the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not.
comments.explanation: The result that appears to hold for any graph is the information-theoretic comparison, namely that the **O**-set has adjustment information at least as large as the Adjust-set. Translating this into smaller or equal asymptotic variance requires the estimator to satisfy relation (8) or relation (9), as the next paragraph correctly acknowledges. I therefore do not think the intended theorem is wrong, but this sentence states the variance conclusion without the necessary estimator-class assumption. For estimators outside that class, the paper’s own discussion and numerical results suggest the variance ordering need not be guaranteed. A more precise statement would say that the **O**-set has higher/equal adjustment information than Adjust for any graph, and hence smaller/equal asymptotic variance for estimators satisfying the stated relation.

comments.title: Numerical experiments are described as demonstrating finite-sample validity too broadly
comments.quote: Practical contributions comprise Python code to construct adjustment sets and check optimality, as well as extensive numerical experiments that demonstrate that the theoretical results also hold for relatively small sample sizes.
comments.explanation: The experiments are clearly valuable, and the surrounding text is careful in many places to distinguish estimator classes and finite-sample behavior. Still, this sentence overstates what simulations can establish. The theoretical results are graphical/asymptotic statements under specified assumptions; finite-sample performance depends on the data-generating distributions, sample sizes, estimator implementation, tuning choices, and adjustment-set cardinalities. The numerical experiments can corroborate or illustrate that the predicted rankings often persist at small sample sizes in the studied configurations, but they do not demonstrate that the theoretical results generally “hold” at small samples. This is a framing issue rather than a flaw in the theory.
